name: Python for Analysts
blurb: Pandas, cleaning and automation for real analysis.

lessons.title: Your First Three Containers: Variables, Lists, and Dicts
lessons.concept:
If you already think in spreadsheets, Python is closer than you fear. A spreadsheet is really just cells (single values), columns (ordered sequences), and named fields (labelled lookups). Python gives you exactly those three, and once you see the mapping, everything downstream, including pandas, becomes easier. A variable is simply a named box: gst_rate = 0.18. You never declare the type; Python reads the value and decides (this is called dynamic typing). The four types you will meet daily are int (500), float (0.18), str ("Mumbai"), and bool (True/False).

A list is an ordered, changeable sequence written in square brackets: cities = ["Mumbai", "Delhi", "Bengaluru"]. Think of it as a single column of your sheet. The one rule that trips up every Excel user: Python counts from zero, so cities[0] is Mumbai and cities[-1] is the last element. Lists can grow (cities.append("Pune")), be sliced (cities[:2]), and sorted. They shine when order matters and you plan to walk through every item.

A dict (dictionary) maps keys to values using curly braces: loan = {"principal": 500000, "rate": 0.09, "tenure_months": 60}. This is one row of a table with named columns, or a mini lookup table. You fetch by name, not position: loan["rate"]. Dicts are the quiet workhorse of analytics, because a pandas DataFrame is essentially a clever collection of these, and JSON from any API arrives as nested dicts and lists. Master these three and you have the vocabulary for the entire track.
lessons.example: Imagine you run analytics for a Kirana store in Pune. Today's sale of five items is naturally a list: sales = [120, 45, 899, 30, 260]. One customer's bill is naturally a dict: {"name": "Anita", "items": 5, "total": 1354, "paid_upi": True}. The GST rate that applies to everything is a single variable: gst_rate = 0.18. Three containers, one shop.
lessons.code:
# variable: one named value; Python infers the type
gst_rate = 0.18              # float
store_name = "Sharma Kirana" # str

# list: an ordered column of values (0-indexed)
cities = ["Mumbai", "Delhi", "Bengaluru"]
print(cities[0])     # Mumbai
print(cities[-1])    # Bengaluru (last)
cities.append("Pune")

# dict: labelled lookup, like one row of a table
loan = {"principal": 500000, "rate": 0.09, "tenure_months": 60}
print(loan["rate"])  # 0.09
lessons.tryit: Create a dict called budget with your monthly rent, groceries, and EMI as three keys with rupee amounts. Then print the total using budget["rent"] + budget["groceries"] + budget["emi"].

mistakes.m: Assuming the first element is at index 1, like Excel's row 1 or a 1-based mindset.
mistakes.fix: Python is zero-indexed: the first item is [0], the second is [1], and the last is [-1]. Say it out loud until it sticks.

mistakes.m: Writing b = a for a list and expecting b to be an independent copy, then wondering why editing b also changed a.
mistakes.fix: b = a makes b a second name for the SAME list. To get a real copy use b = a.copy().

mistakes.m: Storing SKU->price pairs in two parallel lists and searching one to find the other.
mistakes.fix: Use a dict: prices["SKU123"] is an instant key lookup, while scanning a list gets slower as it grows.

quiz.q: For cities = ["Mumbai", "Delhi", "Bengaluru"], what does cities[0] return?
quiz.why: Python indexes from zero, so position 0 is the first element, Mumbai. Position 1 would be Delhi. This zero-based counting runs through slicing, loops, and pandas .iloc, so anchoring it now prevents dozens of off-by-one bugs later.
quiz.approach: Whenever you index a sequence, mentally start counting at 0, and reach for [-1] when you want the last element without knowing the length.

quiz.q: You need to look up a product's price instantly by its SKU code. Which structure fits best?
quiz.why: A dict maps each SKU key straight to its value, so prices["SKU123"] is a direct O(1) lookup. Searching a list means scanning element by element, which slows down as the data grows and forces you to keep a second parallel list of keys.
quiz.approach: When your access pattern is 'given this label, get that value', model it as a dict rather than hunting through a list.

quiz.q: a = [1, 2]; b = a; b.append(3). What is a now?
quiz.why: b = a does not copy the list; it makes b a second reference to the very same list object, so appending through b is visible through a. This shared-reference behaviour is a classic silent bug in data-prep scripts.
quiz.approach: To branch off an independent version, copy explicitly with a.copy() (or list(a)); use plain assignment only when you truly want two names for one object.

lessons.title: Loops and Functions: Stop Doing the Same Thing 40 Times
lessons.concept:
Analyst work is gloriously repetitive: apply the same tax calc to every invoice, the same rename to every column, the same pull to every month's file. A for loop is how you say 'do this for each item'. for city in cities: print(city) walks the list one element at a time. Two things surprise newcomers: Python uses indentation, not braces, to mark the loop's body (four spaces is the convention), and the line that starts the loop ends in a colon. You can loop over lists, over a dict's items (for k, v in loan.items()), or over a count with range(5).

A function packages logic you want to reuse. def gst_amount(price, rate=0.18): return price * rate defines it once; then gst_amount(1200) computes 216 anywhere. rate=0.18 is a default argument, so callers can omit it or override it (gst_amount(1200, 0.05)). The golden habit is return, not print: a function that returns a value can be tested, reused, and fed into the next step, whereas one that only prints is a dead end. This is the DRY principle, Don't Repeat Yourself, and it is what separates a script you trust from one you copy-paste and pray over.

Combine the two and you can process a whole list with a function. Python even offers a crisp shorthand called a list comprehension: [gst_amount(p) for p in prices] builds a new list in one line. A gentle heads-up for the next lesson: inside pandas you will rarely write an explicit row-by-row loop, because vectorized column math is far faster. But loops remain essential for iterating over files, API pages, and any logic that isn't simple column arithmetic, so this muscle stays useful for life.
lessons.example: Your finance team sends invoice amounts for the day: [1200, 4999, 250, 89999]. Instead of computing 18% GST four times by hand, you write gst_amount once and loop, and tomorrow when there are 400 invoices the exact same three lines still work without a single edit.
lessons.code:
prices = [1200, 4999, 250, 89999]

def gst_amount(price, rate=0.18):
    return price * rate          # return, so the value is reusable

# loop: apply the function to each value
for p in prices:
    print(p, "->", gst_amount(p))

# list comprehension: the same work as a Pythonic one-liner
gst = [gst_amount(p) for p in prices]
print(gst)   # [216.0, 899.82, 45.0, 16199.82]
lessons.tryit: Write a function take_home(salary) that returns salary minus a flat 10% deduction. Then loop over [30000, 55000, 82000, 120000] and print each in-hand amount.

mistakes.m: Forgetting the colon after the for/def line, or indenting the body inconsistently.
mistakes.fix: End the header line with a colon and indent the block with a consistent four spaces. Mixing tabs and spaces is the usual cause of IndentationError.

mistakes.m: Writing a function that print()s the result instead of returning it, then being unable to use the value later.
mistakes.fix: return the value; the caller can print it if needed. A returned value can be stored, summed, or charted; a printed one vanishes.

mistakes.m: Adding to or removing from a list while looping over that same list, which skips items.
mistakes.fix: Iterate over a copy (for x in items.copy()) or build a fresh result list instead of mutating the one you are reading.

links.t: Python Tutorial — Control Flow and Functions

quiz.q: Why is it better for a reusable calculation function to use return instead of print?
opts: print is much slower than return
A returned value can be stored, reused, and tested; a printed one cannot
print does not work inside functions
quiz.why: return hands the computed value back to the caller, who can assign it, feed it into another function, or check it in a test. print merely shows text on screen and gives back None, so the result is lost the moment it is displayed.
quiz.approach: Make functions return their results; do any printing at the call site. This keeps logic composable and testable.

quiz.q: What kind of expression is [gst_amount(p) for p in prices]?
opts: A list comprehension that builds a new list
quiz.why: Square brackets wrapping an expression followed by a for clause produce a new list, evaluating gst_amount(p) for every p in prices. It is a compact, readable substitute for a loop that appends to a list.
quiz.approach: Reach for a list comprehension when you are transforming every item of a sequence into a new list; keep an explicit loop when the body has side effects or multiple steps.

quiz.q: Which situation most directly causes an IndentationError?
opts: The lines inside the loop body are not indented consistently
The loop header ends with a colon
You call a function inside the loop
You name a variable the same as a list
quiz.why: Python uses indentation to define which lines belong to the loop or function body, so inconsistent or missing indentation (often tabs mixed with spaces) breaks that structure and raises IndentationError. A colon on the header is required and correct, not an error.
quiz.approach: Pick four spaces per level and never mix tabs with spaces; most editors can show whitespace and auto-convert tabs to spaces.

lessons.title: pandas in Anger: Filter, Group, Join, and Why You Never Loop
lessons.concept:
pandas is a spreadsheet you drive with code, and its central object is the DataFrame, a table of rows and named columns. You load one with import pandas as pd then df = pd.read_csv("sales.csv"). Each column is a Series. Filtering uses a boolean mask: df[df["state"] == "Maharashtra"] keeps only the matching rows. When you combine conditions you must wrap each in parentheses and join with & (and) or | (or): df[(df["state"] == "Maharashtra") & (df["revenue"] > 1000)]. Using Python's plain and/or here throws an error, because pandas compares whole columns at once, not single True/False values.

Grouping is the pivot table you already love. df.groupby("state")["revenue"].sum() splits the data by state, applies sum to revenue, and combines the results, the classic split-apply-combine pattern. Swap sum() for mean(), count(), or .agg(["sum", "mean"]) for several at once. Joining is VLOOKUP done properly: pd.merge(orders, customers, on="customer_id", how="left") stitches two tables on a shared key. The how argument decides who survives: 'left' keeps every order even if a customer is missing, 'inner' keeps only matches, and 'outer' keeps everything from both sides.

The single most important performance idea is vectorization. pandas runs column operations in fast compiled code, so df["gst"] = df["revenue"] * 0.18 computes GST for a million rows in one shot, often 10 to 100 times faster than a hand-written Python loop over rows. This is why you almost never write for i in range(len(df)) in analytics; if you catch yourself looping row by row to compute a column, stop and express it as whole-column math instead. Think in columns, not rows, and pandas rewards you with both speed and shorter, clearer code.
lessons.example: On a used-car marketplace, orders sits in one file and customers in another. You filter to Maharashtra deals above ₹1,000 margin, group revenue by city to find where the business is strongest, and left-join customer age band onto every order, all in a handful of lines that would be a fragile maze of VLOOKUPs and pivot tables in Excel.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("sales.csv")

# filter: boolean mask (parentheses around each condition, & to combine)
mh = df[(df["state"] == "Maharashtra") & (df["revenue"] > 1000)]

# group: split-apply-combine, like a pivot table
by_state = df.groupby("state")["revenue"].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

# join: VLOOKUP done right; keep every order with how="left"
full = pd.merge(orders, customers, on="customer_id", how="left")

# vectorize: whole-column math, no loop needed
df["gst"] = df["revenue"] * 0.18
lessons.tryit: From a sales CSV with columns city and revenue, compute total revenue per city with groupby, sort it descending, and print the top 3 cities using .head(3).

mistakes.m: Combining filters without parentheses, e.g. df[df.a == 1 & df.b == 2], and getting wrong or error-filled results.
mistakes.fix: Wrap each condition in parentheses: df[(df.a == 1) & (df.b == 2)]. The & operator binds tighter than ==, so the parentheses are mandatory.

mistakes.m: Using Python's and / or between two Series conditions.
mistakes.fix: Use the element-wise operators & (and) and | (or). Plain and/or raise 'The truth value of a Series is ambiguous' because pandas can't reduce a whole column to one True/False.

mistakes.m: Looping with df.iterrows() to compute a new column value by value.
mistakes.fix: Express it as vectorized column math (df["gst"] = df["revenue"] * 0.18). It is dramatically faster and shorter than iterating rows.

quiz.q: Which line correctly keeps rows where state is 'MH' AND revenue is above 1000?
quiz.why: Each condition must be parenthesised because & has higher precedence than ==, and conditions on Series must be combined with & rather than the Python keyword and, which can't evaluate a whole column to a single truth value. The parenthesised & form is the only one that is both syntactically valid and correct.
quiz.approach: For pandas masks, always parenthesise each comparison and join them with & or |, never and/or.

quiz.q: df.groupby("state")["revenue"].sum() is most analogous to which Excel feature?
opts: A pivot table summing revenue by state
quiz.why: groupby splits rows by the key, applies an aggregation, and combines the results, which is exactly what a pivot table does when it sums a value field across a row category. VLOOKUP is closer to a merge/join, not an aggregation.
quiz.approach: When you would build a pivot table, reach for groupby plus an aggregation like sum, mean, or count.

quiz.q: What is the fastest, most idiomatic way to add a gst column equal to revenue times 0.18?
opts: Loop with df.iterrows() and assign each row
Use a Python for i in range(len(df)) loop
Compute each value manually in Excel first
quiz.why: Multiplying the whole Series at once is vectorized: pandas runs it in fast compiled code across all rows in a single operation, typically 10 to 100 times faster than iterating rows in Python. iterrows and index loops are slow and verbose by comparison.
quiz.approach: Default to whole-column (vectorized) operations; treat row-by-row loops in pandas as a last resort.

lessons.title: Messy Data to Trustworthy Insight: Cleaning Plus an EDA Checklist
lessons.concept:
Real data arrives dirty, and a beautiful analysis on dirty data is just a confident mistake. Four problems recur. Wrong dtypes: an amount stored as the string "₹1,200" won't sum, it concatenates. Check types with df.info(), then convert using pd.to_numeric, .astype(...), or pd.to_datetime for dates. NULLs (missing values, shown as NaN): count them per column with df.isna().sum(), then decide deliberately whether to drop rows (df.dropna()) or fill them (df.fillna(...)). Duplicates: measure with df.duplicated().sum() and remove with df.drop_duplicates(). Inconsistent text: "Mumbai", "mumbai", and " MUMBAI " are three cities to a computer, so standardise with df["city"].str.strip().str.title().

A subtle but important truth: NaN is not equal to anything, not even itself, so never test for missing with == NaN. Use .isna() instead. Equally, be honest about why a value is missing. Filling every missing revenue with 0 is tempting and usually wrong: a lead whose revenue is unknown is not a lead worth ₹0, and blindly zero-filling will deflate your averages and totals into a misleading story. Sometimes the right move is to drop, sometimes to fill with a median, and sometimes to add a 'was_missing' flag and keep the gap visible.

Once clean, run a quick EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis) checklist before drawing any conclusion: shape (how many rows and columns), head() (eyeball a few rows), info()/dtypes, describe() (min, max, mean, quartiles for numeric columns), value_counts() (frequencies for categoricals), missingness, and obvious outliers. Then look with charts, because the eye catches what a table hides: a histogram (df["revenue"].hist()) reveals a skewed distribution, a bar chart of value_counts shows category balance, and a line chart over months shows trend. Five minutes of this checklist saves you from presenting a number that a stray duplicate or a text-typed amount quietly corrupted.
lessons.example: You export a leads file from your CRM and the amount column reads "₹1,200", city has "Delhi", "delhi" and " DELHI ", and 40 rows are exact duplicates from a double-sync. Run the checklist, fix dtypes, standardise city, drop duplicates, count the NULLs, and only then report that Bengaluru drives the most qualified pipeline.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("leads.csv")

# 1. Look before you leap (the EDA checklist starts here)
print(df.shape)          # (rows, cols)
df.info()                # dtypes + non-null counts
print(df.isna().sum())   # missing values per column

# 2. Fix dtype: "₹1,200" (a string) -> 1200 (a number)
df["amount"] = (df["amount"].str.replace("₹", "", regex=False)
                            .str.replace(",", "", regex=False))
df["amount"] = pd.to_numeric(df["amount"], errors="coerce")

# 3. Standardise text and drop duplicate rows
df["city"] = df["city"].str.strip().str.title()
df = df.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)

# 4. Quick chart to see the distribution
df["amount"].hist()
lessons.tryit: Load any messy CSV you have. Print df.isna().sum() and df.duplicated().sum(), then produce a cleaned DataFrame that fixes one dtype, standardises one text column with .str.strip().str.title(), and drops duplicate rows.

mistakes.m: Filling every missing value with 0 by reflex (df.fillna(0)).
mistakes.fix: Zero is a real number that drags down means and inflates counts. Ask why the value is missing, then choose to drop, fill with a median, or flag it, per column.

mistakes.m: Testing for missing values with df["x"] == NaN and getting an all-False mask.
mistakes.fix: NaN is never equal to anything, including itself. Use df["x"].isna() (and .notna() for the opposite).

mistakes.m: Running a cleaning step like drop_duplicates() but forgetting it returns a new frame, so the original is unchanged.
mistakes.fix: Reassign the result (df = df.drop_duplicates()) or pass inplace=True, and add .reset_index(drop=True) after dropping rows so the index stays tidy.

quiz.q: An amount column holds values like "₹1,200" and df["amount"].sum() gives a long concatenated string instead of a total. Why?
opts: The column's dtype is object (text), not numeric
It is a bug in pandas
quiz.why: Because the values contain a rupee sign and comma, pandas stored the column as text (object dtype), and summing text concatenates rather than adds. Stripping the symbols and calling pd.to_numeric converts it to real numbers so arithmetic works.
quiz.approach: Check df.info() early; when a numeric column shows object dtype, clean the characters and convert with pd.to_numeric before any math.

quiz.q: Which is the standard way to count missing values in each column?
quiz.why: df.isna() produces a True/False table of where values are missing, and .sum() adds the Trues column by column to give a per-column NaN count. df.count() counts non-missing values, describe() summarises numeric distributions, and dropna() removes rows rather than counting.
quiz.approach: Start every cleaning pass with df.isna().sum() to see exactly where and how much data is missing before deciding what to do.

quiz.q: Why is filling all missing revenue values with 0 risky?
opts: pandas raises an error when you do it
It understates averages and totals, telling a misleading story
0 is not a valid number in pandas
quiz.why: A missing revenue means 'unknown', not 'zero'. Replacing unknowns with 0 pulls the mean down and can distort sums and comparisons, so the analysis looks precise but is quietly wrong. Dropping, median-filling, or flagging are more honest choices depending on context.
quiz.approach: Treat missingness as a decision, not a default: understand why the value is absent, then pick drop, a justified fill, or an explicit missing-flag.

lessons.title: Filter, Group, Join: The Three Verbs That Run 80% of Analytics
lessons.concept:
Most analyst work in pandas is just three verbs applied over and over: filter rows, group-and-aggregate, and join tables. Master these and you have replaced the majority of manual Excel pivots. The mental model is a table (a DataFrame) that you slice, roll up, and stitch to other tables. In an Excel VLOOKUP you match one key at a time; pandas does the whole column at once, which is why it is faster and why 'vectorization' matters.

Filtering uses a boolean mask: df[df['city'] == 'Bengaluru'] keeps only rows where the condition is True. Combine conditions with & (and) and | (or), and wrap each condition in parentheses because Python's operator precedence will otherwise mis-parse them. Grouping follows the split-apply-combine idea: df.groupby('city')['amount'].sum() splits rows by city, applies sum to the amount column, and combines the results into one number per city. You can aggregate several ways at once with .agg({'amount':'sum','order_id':'count'}).

Joining is pandas' VLOOKUP on steroids. df.merge(other, on='customer_id', how='left') keeps every row on the left and pulls matching columns from the right. The how argument is the whole game: 'inner' keeps only matches, 'left' keeps all left rows (matches or not), 'outer' keeps everything. The single biggest cause of wrong numbers is a merge that silently duplicates rows because the key is not unique on the right side — always check row counts before and after.
lessons.example: You are an analyst at a Gurugram fintech. You have a transactions table (5 lakh rows: order_id, customer_id, amount, city) and a customers table (customer_id, segment). Leadership asks: 'What is total GMV per city for our Premium customers only?' That single question needs all three verbs — join transactions to customers to get segment, filter to Premium, then group by city and sum amount.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

# txns: order_id, customer_id, amount, city
# custs: customer_id, segment

# 1) JOIN to bring segment onto each transaction
df = txns.merge(custs, on='customer_id', how='left')

# 2) FILTER to Premium (note the parentheses)
prem = df[df['segment'] == 'Premium']

# 3) GROUP + aggregate
gmv = (prem.groupby('city')['amount']
            .sum()
            .sort_values(ascending=False))
print(gmv)

# Multi-metric version
summary = (prem.groupby('city')
               .agg(gmv=('amount','sum'),
                    orders=('order_id','count'),
                    avg_ticket=('amount','mean'))
               .reset_index())
lessons.tryit: Take any CSV with a category column and an amount column. Compute total and average amount per category in one .agg() call, then sort descending. Bonus: filter to only categories where total amount exceeds ₹1,00,000 using .loc after grouping.

mistakes.m: Writing df[df['a']==1 & df['b']==2] without parentheses around each condition.
mistakes.fix: Always wrap: df[(df['a']==1) & (df['b']==2)]. The & operator binds tighter than ==, so missing parentheses raises an error or gives wrong results. Use & and | (not the words 'and'/'or') for element-wise comparison.

mistakes.m: Merging on a key that is not unique on the right table, silently multiplying rows and inflating totals.
mistakes.fix: Before trusting a merge, run other['key'].duplicated().sum() to confirm uniqueness, and compare len(df) before and after. Pass validate='m:1' to merge so pandas raises an error if the relationship is not many-to-one.

mistakes.m: Using how='inner' by default and quietly dropping customers who had no matching row.
mistakes.fix: Decide intent first. If you must keep every transaction even when the customer lookup fails, use how='left', then check for NaN in the joined column to count unmatched rows.

quiz.q: You run txns.merge(custs, on='customer_id', how='left') and the row count jumps from 5,00,000 to 5,40,000. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The left join dropped unmatched rows
customer_id is duplicated in the custs table, so some transactions matched multiple customer rows
quiz.why: A left join never adds rows because of the left table — it keeps each left row once. Extra rows appear when the RIGHT (custs) key is not unique: a single transaction matches several customer rows and gets duplicated. That is exactly why you check right-key uniqueness before merging.
quiz.approach: Before any merge, verify the join key is unique on the side you are looking up, and compare row counts before and after. Use validate='m:1' to make pandas enforce it.

quiz.q: Which expression correctly keeps rows where city is 'Pune' AND amount is above 5000?
quiz.why: Element-wise boolean logic in pandas needs & (not the Python keyword 'and', which works on single True/False values, not arrays), and each comparison must be parenthesised because & has higher precedence than ==. Option B satisfies both rules.
quiz.approach: For row masks use & / | with parentheses around every condition; reserve the words 'and'/'or' for plain scalars.

quiz.q: What does df.groupby('city')['amount'].sum() return?
opts: The overall sum of amount across all cities
One summed amount per city, indexed by city
The count of rows per city
A filtered DataFrame of the highest city
quiz.why: groupby('city') splits rows into one group per city, ['amount'] selects the column, and .sum() aggregates within each group — producing a Series with one total per city (city as the index). This is the split-apply-combine pattern.
quiz.approach: Read groupby chains left to right as split (by key), select (column), apply (aggregation). Add .reset_index() when you want a flat DataFrame instead of an indexed Series.

lessons.title: Vectorization: Why the Loop You Wrote Runs 100x Too Slow
lessons.concept:
New analysts instinctively write a for-loop over rows: 'for each row, compute GST and add it to a new column.' In pandas this is almost always the wrong reflex. pandas columns are backed by NumPy arrays, and NumPy runs arithmetic on the whole array in compiled C code in one shot. This is vectorization: instead of Python visiting 5 lakh rows one at a time, the operation df['amount'] * 1.18 is executed at hardware speed on the entire column. The difference is not 10% — it is routinely 50x to 200x, turning a two-minute job into under a second.

The practical rule: avoid iterrows() and avoid apply() with a Python function whenever a vectorized equivalent exists. Arithmetic (+, -, *, /), comparisons, and most string and datetime operations all have column-wide forms. For conditional logic, reach for np.where(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false) for a single yes/no split, or np.select([cond1, cond2], [val1, val2], default=...) for multiple bands — both operate on the entire column at once. For row-wise text you use the .str accessor (df['name'].str.upper()) and for dates the .dt accessor (df['date'].dt.month).

When do you still loop? Rarely. If a computation genuinely depends on the previous row's result (a running custom state machine) or you are calling an external API per row, a loop is acceptable — but even then, try .cumsum() / .shift() / .rolling() first. The instinct to fix: if you catch yourself typing 'for index, row in df.iterrows()', pause and ask 'what is the column-level operation here?'
lessons.example: An analyst at a D2C brand needs to add 18% GST to 8 lakh order lines and flag orders above ₹10,000 as 'High Value'. Written as an iterrows loop it takes ~90 seconds on a laptop; written vectorized with df['amount']*1.18 and np.where it finishes in well under a second — and the code is shorter and easier to read.
lessons.code:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# SLOW: row-by-row Python loop (avoid)
# for i, row in df.iterrows():
#     df.at[i, 'total'] = row['amount'] * 1.18

# FAST: whole-column arithmetic
df['total'] = df['amount'] * 1.18

# Single condition -> np.where
df['tier'] = np.where(df['amount'] > 10000, 'High Value', 'Standard')

# Multiple bands -> np.select
conds = [df['amount'] < 1000,
         df['amount'] < 10000]
labels = ['Small', 'Medium']
df['band'] = np.select(conds, labels, default='Large')

# Vectorized string + datetime
df['city'] = df['city'].str.strip().str.title()
df['month'] = pd.to_datetime(df['order_date']).dt.month
lessons.tryit: Take a DataFrame with an 'amount' column. Create a 'total_with_gst' column using vectorized multiplication, then time it against an iterrows loop doing the same thing using Python's time module. Print both durations and see the ratio yourself.

mistakes.m: Believing df.apply(func, axis=1) is 'the pandas way' and therefore fast.
mistakes.fix: apply with axis=1 is essentially a disguised Python loop — it calls your function once per row. It is fine for convenience on small data, but for real volume replace it with column arithmetic, np.where, or np.select, which run in compiled code.

mistakes.m: Using iterrows() to build a new column value by value.
mistakes.fix: Almost every iterrows pattern has a vectorized form. Express the logic as an operation on whole columns. Reserve loops for genuinely sequential logic that cannot be vectorized, and even then try .shift()/.cumsum() first.

mistakes.m: Chaining if/elif logic inside a Python function and applying it, instead of using np.select.
mistakes.fix: Multi-way conditional assignment is exactly what np.select is for: pass a list of conditions and a matching list of values plus a default. It evaluates the whole column at once.

quiz.q: Why is df['a'] * 1.18 typically far faster than looping over rows to multiply each value?
opts: Because pandas caches the result to disk
Because the whole-column operation runs in compiled C over a NumPy array instead of interpreting Python once per row
Because multiplication is a special-cased shortcut only for 1.18
Because it uses multiple CPU cores by default
quiz.why: pandas columns sit on NumPy arrays, so column-wide arithmetic executes as a single compiled C loop rather than the Python interpreter processing each element individually. That compiled path, not caching or GST-specific tricks, is the source of the speed-up.
quiz.approach: Prefer whole-column (vectorized) operations; think in columns, not rows. Interpreted per-row Python is the default cause of slow pandas code.

quiz.q: You need to label amounts as 'Small' (<1000), 'Medium' (<10000), else 'Large'. What is the cleanest vectorized approach?
opts: A for-loop with if/elif/else appending to a list
df.apply with a Python function checking bands
Three separate np.where calls overwriting each other blindly
quiz.why: np.select is purpose-built for multi-way conditional assignment: it takes an ordered list of conditions and matching values plus a default, and evaluates the entire column in compiled code. It reads clearly and scales. The loop and apply are per-row Python; nested np.where works but gets unreadable fast.
quiz.approach: One condition -> np.where; several ordered bands -> np.select. Both keep you vectorized and avoid row loops.

quiz.q: When is a Python loop over rows genuinely defensible in pandas?
opts: Whenever the logic has an if statement
When each row's result truly depends on prior rows' computed state or you must call an external service per row
When the DataFrame has more than a million rows
quiz.why: Vectorization handles conditionals fine (np.where/np.select), so an if statement alone is no excuse. A loop is defensible only for genuinely sequential state that vectorized tools like shift/cumsum can't express, or side-effectful per-row work like an API call. Even then, try vectorized options first.
quiz.approach: Default to vectorized. Loop only for true sequential dependencies or per-row side effects — and first check whether shift/cumsum/rolling already solve it.

lessons.title: Cleaning Messy Data: Dtypes, NULLs, and Duplicates Without Fooling Yourself
lessons.concept:
Raw data lies to you in three recurring ways, and each has a specific fix. First, dtypes: a column that looks like numbers may actually be text. If 'amount' arrives as strings like '1,200' or '₹500', df['amount'].sum() will concatenate or error instead of adding. Always run df.dtypes early; a numeric column showing 'object' is a red flag. Convert with pd.to_numeric(df['amount'], errors='coerce') — the errors='coerce' turns unparseable junk into NaN instead of crashing, which is usually what you want so one bad row doesn't stop the whole job. Dates are the same story: pd.to_datetime, and beware that Indian data is often DD/MM/YYYY while pandas defaults to month-first, so pass dayfirst=True.

Second, NULLs. In pandas, missing values are NaN (or NaT for dates, pd.NA for newer nullable types). The trap is that NaN != NaN, so you never test x == NaN; you use df['col'].isna(). Decide deliberately per column: drop rows (dropna), fill with a sensible default (fillna(0) for counts, a median for continuous values), or leave them and let aggregations skip them — note that sum() and mean() ignore NaN by default, which can quietly change your denominator. 'Missing' is information; don't erase it thoughtlessly.

Third, duplicates. df.duplicated().sum() counts fully identical rows, but real duplicates are usually partial — the same order_id appearing twice with a different timestamp. Use subset to define what 'same' means: df.drop_duplicates(subset=['order_id'], keep='last'). Before dropping, always inspect: df[df.duplicated(subset=['order_id'], keep=False)] shows every copy so you can confirm they are truly redundant and choose which to keep. Cleaning is not about making warnings disappear; it is about making the numbers trustworthy.
lessons.example: You pull a UPI transactions export from a partner. The 'amount' column is text like '1,250.00', dates are '05/07/2026' (5 July, not May), 3% of rows have a blank customer_id, and some order_ids repeat because of a retry bug. Before you can report GMV, all three problems must be handled — or your total will be silently wrong.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

# Inspect first — never trust, verify
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.isna().sum())          # NULLs per column
print(df.duplicated(subset=['order_id']).sum())

# 1) Fix dtypes
df['amount'] = (df['amount'].astype(str)
                              .str.replace(',', '', regex=False)
                              .str.replace('₹', '', regex=False))
df['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(df['amount'], errors='coerce')
df['order_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['order_date'], dayfirst=True, errors='coerce')

# 2) Handle NULLs deliberately
df = df.dropna(subset=['customer_id'])   # can't attribute revenue without it
df['coupon'] = df['coupon'].fillna('NONE')

# 3) De-duplicate the retry rows, keeping the latest
dupes = df[df.duplicated(subset=['order_id'], keep=False)]
print('Inspect these before dropping:', len(dupes))
df = df.sort_values('order_date').drop_duplicates(subset=['order_id'], keep='last')
lessons.tryit: Take a messy CSV. In order: print df.dtypes and df.isna().sum(), convert one text-number column with pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce'), count how many became NaN, then de-duplicate on a business key with keep='last'. Write down the row count after each step.

mistakes.m: Testing for missing values with df[df['col'] == None] or == NaN.
mistakes.fix: NaN is never equal to anything, including itself. Use df['col'].isna() (or .notna()). For filling, use .fillna(value); for dropping, .dropna(subset=[...]).

mistakes.m: Letting pd.to_datetime parse Indian DD/MM dates as month-first, turning 05/07 into 5 May instead of 5 July.
mistakes.fix: Pass dayfirst=True for day-first formats, and prefer an explicit format= string when you know it. Always spot-check a few parsed dates against the source.

mistakes.m: Calling drop_duplicates() with no subset and assuming duplicates are gone.
mistakes.fix: Bare drop_duplicates only removes fully identical rows. Real-world duplicates differ in a timestamp or ID column. Define the business key with subset=[...] and choose keep='first'/'last' after inspecting the duplicate rows with keep=False.

quiz.q: A column of amounts imported as strings like '1,200'. What does pd.to_numeric(df['amount'], errors='coerce') do to a value like 'N/A'?
opts: Raises an error and stops the whole conversion
Converts it to NaN and continues with the rest
quiz.why: errors='coerce' means 'if a value can't be parsed as a number, make it NaN rather than crashing.' So 'N/A' becomes NaN and the other rows convert normally. (Note: you'd still strip the comma first so '1,200' parses to 1200.)
quiz.approach: Use errors='coerce' to survive dirty inputs, then inspect how many NaNs were produced with .isna().sum() to gauge data quality — don't assume the coercion was harmless.

quiz.q: Which is the correct way to keep only rows where 'customer_id' is present?
quiz.why: Because NaN is not equal to anything (including NaN), comparison operators can't detect it. .notna() is the dedicated test for 'has a value', so df[df['customer_id'].notna()] keeps the populated rows. (dropna(subset=['customer_id']) is the equivalent shortcut.)
quiz.approach: Detect missingness with isna()/notna(), never with == None or == NaN. Comparisons silently fail on missing values.

quiz.q: An order table has each order_id twice due to a payment-retry bug, differing only in timestamp. Which reliably keeps one row per order — the latest?
opts: df.drop_duplicates(subset=['order_id'], keep='last') after sorting by timestamp
quiz.why: Bare drop_duplicates only removes rows identical in every column, so the differing timestamps defeat it. Defining the business key with subset=['order_id'] and keep='last' (after sorting by time) keeps exactly one row per order — the most recent. dropna handles nulls, not dupes, and df[df.duplicated()] returns the extras rather than removing them.
quiz.approach: Always define what 'duplicate' means with subset, inspect the duplicate set with keep=False first, then drop with an explicit keep rule.

lessons.title: From EDA Checklist to Automated Pull: Charts and APIs That Save You Hours
lessons.concept:
Before you model or report anything, run a fixed EDA (exploratory data analysis) checklist so surprises surface early. A dependable five-step pass: (1) shape and dtypes — df.shape and df.dtypes to know size and types; (2) a peek — df.head() and df.sample(5) to see real values; (3) numeric summary — df.describe() to catch impossible min/max like a negative age or a ₹0 order; (4) missingness — df.isna().mean() for the fraction null per column; (5) categorical sanity — df['city'].value_counts() to spot 'Bengaluru' vs 'bengaluru' vs 'Blr'. This checklist takes two minutes and prevents hours of chasing wrong numbers downstream. Pair it with two quick charts: a histogram (df['amount'].hist()) to see distribution and outliers, and a bar chart of a groupby result to see which categories dominate. The goal is orientation, not beauty.

Once you trust the data, the next leverage is automation: pulling data from APIs instead of waiting for someone to email a CSV. The requests library is the workhorse. r = requests.get(url, params={...}, timeout=10) fetches JSON; then the single most important line is r.raise_for_status(), which throws an exception on a 4xx/5xx response so a silent failure (like an expired key returning a 401) doesn't sneak fake data into your report. Read the body with r.json() and load records straight into pandas with pd.DataFrame(r.json()['data']).

Two professional habits make API automation safe. First, never hardcode secrets: read API keys from environment variables (os.environ), not from the notebook, so you don't leak them when sharing code. Second, respect the service: set a timeout so a hung server can't freeze your job, check the API's rate limits, and page through large results rather than demanding everything at once. Automation is where an analyst stops being a report-runner and becomes someone whose dashboards refresh themselves.
lessons.example: You maintain a daily sales dashboard at a Gurugram startup. Instead of asking the ops team to export a CSV every morning, you hit the internal orders API with requests, call raise_for_status() so a bad response is caught loudly, load the JSON into a DataFrame, run your EDA checklist to confirm nothing looks off, and chart yesterday's GMV — the whole refresh runs in seconds on a schedule.
lessons.code:
import os
import requests
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# --- Automated pull ---
API_KEY = os.environ['ORDERS_API_KEY']   # never hardcode secrets
url = 'https://api.internal.example.com/v1/orders'
r = requests.get(url,
                 params={'date': '2026-07-17'},
                 headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {API_KEY}'},
                 timeout=10)
r.raise_for_status()                      # fail loudly on 4xx/5xx
df = pd.DataFrame(r.json()['data'])

# --- EDA checklist ---
print(df.shape)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.describe())
print(df.isna().mean().round(3))          # fraction null per column
print(df['city'].value_counts().head())

# --- Quick charts ---
df['amount'].hist(bins=30)
plt.title('Order amount distribution'); plt.show()

(df.groupby('city')['amount'].sum()
   .sort_values().plot(kind='barh'))
plt.title('GMV by city'); plt.show()
lessons.tryit: Use the free public API https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas . Do requests.get with timeout=10, call raise_for_status(), then print r.json()['stargazers_count']. Next, deliberately request a repo that doesn't exist and observe how raise_for_status() turns the 404 into a clear exception instead of silent bad data.

mistakes.m: Calling requests.get and immediately using r.json() without checking the status code.
mistakes.fix: Always call r.raise_for_status() right after the request. A 401/404/500 often still returns a body; without the status check you may load an error page or empty payload as if it were real data.

mistakes.m: Skipping EDA and jumping straight to aggregation, then reporting a mean skewed by an outlier or a wrong dtype.
mistakes.fix: Run the fixed checklist first: shape, dtypes, describe, isna().mean(), value_counts(). describe() alone catches negative amounts, impossible maxima, and columns silently stored as text.

mistakes.m: Hardcoding the API key in the notebook and omitting a timeout.
mistakes.fix: Read secrets from os.environ so they never get committed or shared, and always pass timeout= so a non-responding server can't hang your script indefinitely.

quiz.q: What is the main purpose of r.raise_for_status() after requests.get()?
opts: It converts the response body to a DataFrame
It raises an exception if the HTTP status indicates an error (4xx/5xx) so failures are caught, not silently ingested
It automatically retries the request three times
quiz.why: raise_for_status() inspects the HTTP status code and throws an exception for error responses (like 401 or 500). Without it, an error response still has a body, and you might load an error message or empty payload as if it were valid data. It does not parse, speed up, or retry.
quiz.approach: Treat raise_for_status() as a mandatory guard right after every request, so bad responses fail loudly instead of contaminating your analysis.

quiz.q: During EDA, which single command most quickly reveals an impossible value like a negative order amount or a column secretly stored as text?
quiz.why: df.describe() shows count, mean, min, max, and quartiles for numeric columns — so a negative min or an absurd max jumps out immediately. It also only summarizes numeric columns by default, so a numeric-looking column missing from the output signals it's stored as text. head() shows values but not ranges; shape and columns show structure, not distribution.
quiz.approach: Make describe() a fixed step in your EDA checklist to catch range and dtype problems in one glance, then follow up with isna().mean() and value_counts().

quiz.q: Why read an API key with os.environ['API_KEY'] instead of writing it directly in the notebook?
opts: It keeps the secret out of shared/committed code so it isn't leaked
pandas can only read keys from the environment
It automatically rotates the key daily
quiz.why: Hardcoding a key means anyone you share the notebook or repo with can see and misuse it. Reading from os.environ keeps the secret in the runtime environment, not in the code, so sharing the file doesn't expose the credential. It has no effect on speed, rotation, or pandas' abilities.
quiz.approach: Keep all secrets in environment variables (or a secrets manager), never in source. Also set a timeout on every request so a hung server can't freeze your job.

lessons.title: Think in Columns, Not Rows: Vectorized Filter, Group, Join
lessons.concept:
The single biggest upgrade an analyst can make is to stop thinking in rows and start thinking in columns. In pandas, a column is a NumPy array sitting in one contiguous block of memory. When you write cars["margin"] = cars["sale_price"] - cars["buy_price"], the subtraction runs once, in compiled C, over all 50,000 values at the same time. That is vectorization. The equivalent Python for-loop does the same arithmetic but pays the interpreter tax on every single row, so it is typically 50-200x slower and far easier to get wrong. Rule of thumb: if you are writing iterrows() or a for-loop to build a column, there is almost always a one-line vectorized version.

Three verbs cover most daily work. FILTER is a boolean mask: cars[(cars.city=="Delhi") & (cars.body=="SUV")] keeps only the True rows. GROUP is split-apply-combine: cars.groupby("city").agg(...) collapses lakhs of rows into one row per city with any summary you want. JOIN is a SQL join by another name: cars.merge(targets, on="city", how="left") glues a lookup table onto your data. how="left" keeps every left row (the default is "inner", which silently drops non-matches).

The join is where careful analysts and careless ones part ways. If the right-hand table has duplicate keys, every matching left row is multiplied, so a 50,000-row frame can quietly balloon to 90,000 and every sum you report is now inflated. Two habits protect you: check the row count before and after, and pass validate="many_to_one" (or "one_to_one") so pandas raises an error the moment the key cardinality is not what you assumed.
lessons.example: You have 50,000 Cars24 listings and need average dealer margin by city. A boolean mask pulls out Delhi SUVs, groupby("city") collapses the whole file into a ~20-row city table in milliseconds, and a left-join to a city-level sales-target table tells you which cities beat plan, all without writing a single for-loop.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

cars = pd.read_csv("listings.csv")   # 50,000 used-car rows

# SLOW (avoid): Python loop, one row at a time
# margins = []
# for i in range(len(cars)):
#     margins.append(cars.loc[i, "sale_price"] - cars.loc[i, "buy_price"])

# FAST: vectorized, whole columns at C speed
cars["margin"] = cars["sale_price"] - cars["buy_price"]

# FILTER with a boolean mask ( & | ~ , each side in parentheses )
delhi_suv = cars[(cars["city"] == "Delhi") & (cars["body_type"] == "SUV")]

# GROUP: avg margin and listing count per city
summary = (cars.groupby("city")
                .agg(avg_margin=("margin", "mean"),
                     listings=("margin", "size"))
                .sort_values("avg_margin", ascending=False))

# JOIN a city-level target table; validate guards against row explosion
cars = cars.merge(city_targets, on="city", how="left", validate="many_to_one")
lessons.tryit: Take any CSV with a category column and two numeric columns. Create a derived column with pure column arithmetic (no loop), then produce a groupby table with two aggregations (mean and count) sorted descending. Note how many lines it took versus a loop.

mistakes.m: Joining two columns of conditions with Python's and / or, e.g. df[df.a>0 and df.b>0].
mistakes.fix: Use the bitwise operators & | ~ with each condition wrapped in parentheses: df[(df.a>0) & (df.b>0)]. and/or try to collapse a whole Series to one True/False and raise 'ambiguous truth value'.

mistakes.m: Using iterrows() or a for-loop to compute a new column.
mistakes.fix: Do column arithmetic directly (df['c']=df['a']*df['b']) or use np.where / .map for conditional logic. Vectorized code is shorter and dramatically faster.

mistakes.m: Assuming merge preserves your row count; a left join on a right table with duplicate keys silently multiplies rows.
mistakes.fix: Compare df.shape before and after, and pass validate='many_to_one' (or 'one_to_one') so pandas errors out when keys are not as unique as you assumed.

links.t: pandas User Guide: Merge, join, concatenate
NumPy: What is NumPy (why vectorization is fast)

quiz.q: Why must you write df[(df.a>0) & (df.b>0)] instead of df[df.a>0 and df.b>0]?
opts: & is applied element-wise across the whole boolean Series; Python's and tries to reduce a Series to a single truth value and raises an ambiguity error
and works but is slower than &
& is only valid for integer columns
There is no real difference; both give the same result
quiz.why: and/or evaluate the truthiness of an entire object, which is undefined for a multi-value Series, so pandas raises 'The truth value of a Series is ambiguous'. The bitwise & compares the two boolean Series position by position; parentheses are needed because & binds tighter than the comparison operators.
quiz.approach: Boolean masks use & | ~, and every individual condition goes in its own parentheses.

quiz.q: In cars.merge(targets, on="city", how="left", validate="many_to_one"), what does validate protect against?
opts: It asserts the right table's keys are unique, so matching rows don't unexpectedly multiply (row explosion)
It makes the join run faster
It automatically converts the join key's dtype
It drops rows where the key is null
quiz.why: validate checks the cardinality of the join keys. 'many_to_one' asserts that many left rows map to at most one right row, i.e. the right key is unique. If duplicates exist, pandas raises immediately instead of silently inflating your row count and every downstream total.
quiz.approach: State your join's expected cardinality with validate so accidental fan-out fails loudly, not silently.

quiz.q: Which builds a new price*1.1 column fastest on a 500,000-row DataFrame?
opts: A for-loop over df.index assigning one cell at a time
Looping over df.iterrows() and appending to a list
quiz.why: Column arithmetic runs in compiled C over the entire array at once. apply(axis=1) and iterrows both fall back to a Python-level loop over rows, paying interpreter overhead per row, so they are typically one to two orders of magnitude slower.
quiz.approach: Reach for whole-column operations first; treat apply(axis=1) and iterrows as last resorts.

lessons.title: Taming Messy Data: dtypes, NULLs, and De-duplication
lessons.concept:
Real files never arrive clean. A dealer's export shows price as '₹1,20,000', a registration date as '05/03/2024', a city as ' delhi ', 'Delhi', and 'DELHI' in different rows, and the same VIN twice. Before you can filter, group, or join, you must fix three things: the dtype (is this column numbers, text, or dates?), the NULLs (what does missing mean and how do you handle it?), and the duplicates (which row is the real one?). A trustworthy report and a wrong one differ only in whether this step was done.

dtypes first. A column that looks numeric but contains ₹ signs or commas is stored as object (text), so any arithmetic silently fails or errors. The reliable pattern is: strip the symbols with .str.replace(), then convert with pd.to_numeric(series, errors="coerce"). The magic is errors="coerce": anything unparseable becomes NaN instead of crashing the whole job, so one bad cell doesn't kill 80,000 good rows. For Indian dates, pd.to_datetime(series, dayfirst=True) is essential, because pandas otherwise assumes US month-first order and turns 5 March into 3 May.

NULLs and duplicates close the loop. In pandas, missing values are NaN (a float), so a single missing entry can quietly turn an integer column into floats; use .fillna() with a sensible value (often the median for skewed fields like km_driven) or the nullable Int64 dtype if you must keep whole numbers. For duplicates, a bare drop_duplicates() only removes fully identical rows, which almost never matches your intent. Deduplicate on the business key with subset=, and sort first so keep="last" (or "first") keeps the record you actually want, for example the most recent listing per VIN.
lessons.example: A dealer sends a CSV where price reads '₹1,20,000', reg_date is '05/03/2024', city appears as ' delhi ', 'Delhi', and 'DELHI', and one VIN is listed twice. Converting price to a real number, the date to a real datetime, the city to one canonical spelling, and collapsing to one row per VIN is exactly what makes the downstream group-by numbers correct.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.read_csv("dealer_dump.csv", dtype=str)   # read everything as text first

# 1) Price: '₹1,20,000' -> 120000.0
raw["price"] = (raw["price"]
                .str.replace("₹", "", regex=False)
                .str.replace(",", "", regex=False)
                .str.strip())
raw["price"] = pd.to_numeric(raw["price"], errors="coerce")   # junk -> NaN, no crash

# 2) Indian date: '05/03/2024' = 5 March, not 3 May
raw["reg_date"] = pd.to_datetime(raw["reg_date"], dayfirst=True, errors="coerce")

# 3) Canonicalise text keys BEFORE grouping/joining
raw["city"] = raw["city"].str.strip().str.title()   # ' delhi ' / 'DELHI' -> 'Delhi'

# 4) Fill a skewed numeric field with the median
raw["km_driven"] = pd.to_numeric(raw["km_driven"], errors="coerce")
raw["km_driven"] = raw["km_driven"].fillna(raw["km_driven"].median())

# 5) One row per VIN, keeping the most recent record
clean = (raw.sort_values("reg_date")
            .drop_duplicates(subset="vin", keep="last"))
lessons.tryit: Take a messy column that mixes currency symbols and commas. Clean it to a real numeric dtype with pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce'), then count how many values became NaN with clean['col'].isna().sum() to see how much junk was hiding.

mistakes.m: Calling astype(int) or astype(float) directly on a raw price column like '₹1,20,000'.
mistakes.fix: Strip the symbol and commas first, then use pd.to_numeric(..., errors='coerce') so unparseable values become NaN instead of raising and killing the job.

mistakes.m: Parsing Indian dates without dayfirst, so '05/03/2024' is read as 3 May instead of 5 March.
mistakes.fix: Pass dayfirst=True (with errors='coerce'), or give an explicit format='%d/%m/%Y' to pd.to_datetime.

mistakes.m: Deduplicating with a bare drop_duplicates() that only removes fully identical rows.
mistakes.fix: Deduplicate on the business key with subset=, and sort_values first so keep='last'/'first' retains the record you actually intend to keep.

links.t: pandas User Guide: Working with missing data
pandas User Guide: Time series / date functionality
pandas User Guide: Working with text data

quiz.q: What is the safest way to turn a column of values like '₹1,20,000' into numbers?
opts: Remove ₹ and commas with .str.replace(), then pd.to_numeric(series, errors='coerce')
quiz.why: The column is text because of the symbol and commas, so numeric conversion must first strip those characters. pd.to_numeric with errors='coerce' then turns any remaining unparseable cell into NaN rather than raising, so one malformed value doesn't abort the entire conversion.
quiz.approach: Clean the string, then coerce; errors='coerce' isolates bad cells as NaN instead of crashing.

quiz.q: A column has the Indian-format date '05/03/2024' meaning 5 March 2024. Which parses it correctly?
opts: Leave it as a string and compare alphabetically
quiz.why: By default pandas interprets ambiguous slash dates in month-first (US) order, so '05/03/2024' would become 3 May. dayfirst=True forces day-month-year, giving 5 March. An explicit format='%d/%m/%Y' would work too.
quiz.approach: For DD/MM/YYYY Indian data always set dayfirst=True (or an explicit format) so months and days aren't swapped.

quiz.q: You want exactly one row per VIN, keeping the most recent listing. Which is correct?
quiz.why: A bare drop_duplicates() removes only fully identical rows, not repeats of the same VIN with different details. Sorting by date first and then dropping duplicates on subset='vin' with keep='last' deterministically retains the latest record for each VIN.
quiz.approach: Sort by the tie-breaker, then drop_duplicates on the true key with an explicit keep.

lessons.title: The 2-Minute EDA Checklist and Charts That Answer Questions
lessons.concept:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the disciplined first look you take before trusting a dataset. The goal is not pretty charts; it is to answer 'can I believe this file, and where are the landmines?' A good analyst runs the same short checklist on every new dump so nothing surprises them later. Think of it as a pre-flight check: shape (how big?), info/dtypes (are columns the type I expect?), describe (what are the ranges and are they sane?), missingness (which columns are half-empty?), duplicates (is each row unique?), cardinality via nunique and value_counts (how many cities, what's the dominant fuel type?), and correlation (which numerics move together?).

Two lines catch most disasters. df.isna().mean() returns the fraction of missing values per column, because isna() gives True/False which mean() averages into a proportion, so you instantly see that km_driven is 12% empty. df.duplicated().sum() counts exact duplicate rows. And during EDA always use value_counts(dropna=False) so missing categories are visible; the default hides NaN and can lull you into thinking a column is complete.

Charts turn the checklist into intuition, and each chart type answers a specific question. A histogram answers 'what does one numeric variable's distribution look like, and is it skewed?' A horizontal bar chart answers 'how does a metric compare across categories?' A scatter plot answers 'do these two numerics relate?' A box plot answers 'where are the outliers?' pandas ships a .plot() shortcut over matplotlib, so df["price"].plot(kind="hist") is often all you need. Note that in pandas 2.x, df.corr() no longer auto-drops text columns, so pass numeric_only=True or you'll get a ValueError.
lessons.example: A fresh 80,000-row used-car dump lands in your inbox at month start. Before any analysis you run the same eight-line first-look: shape, dtypes, describe, percent missing, duplicate count, top cities, fuel-type spread, and correlations. In two minutes you learn km_driven is 12% missing, three cities dominate half the inventory, and price and km_driven are negatively correlated as expected.
lessons.code:
# --- The 8-line first look ---
df.shape                          # (rows, cols)
df.info()                         # dtypes + non-null counts per column
df.describe(include="all")        # numeric AND categorical summary
df.isna().mean().round(3)         # FRACTION missing per column
df.duplicated().sum()             # count of exact-duplicate rows
df["city"].value_counts(dropna=False).head(10)   # top cities, NaN shown
df["fuel"].nunique()              # how many distinct fuel types
df.corr(numeric_only=True)        # linear relationships (pandas 2.x needs this flag)

# --- Quick charts (matplotlib via pandas .plot) ---
df["price"].plot(kind="hist", bins=40, title="Price distribution")
df.groupby("city")["margin"].mean().sort_values().plot(kind="barh")
df.plot(kind="scatter", x="km_driven", y="price", alpha=0.3)
lessons.tryit: On any dataset, run df.isna().mean().sort_values(ascending=False) and df.duplicated().sum() back to back. Write down the single most-missing column and whether any exact duplicate rows exist, before you touch the data further.

mistakes.m: Trusting df.describe() alone and skipping the missingness and duplicate checks.
mistakes.fix: Always run df.isna().mean() and df.duplicated().sum() too. describe() silently ignores NaN, so a column that is 40% empty still shows a tidy-looking mean.

mistakes.m: Reading value_counts() and assuming it shows every value; it drops NaN by default.
mistakes.fix: Use value_counts(dropna=False) during EDA so missing entries appear as their own line instead of vanishing.

mistakes.m: Calling df.corr() in pandas 2.x on a frame that still has text columns and hitting a ValueError.
mistakes.fix: Pass numeric_only=True, or select the numeric columns first with df.select_dtypes('number').

links.t: pandas User Guide: Essential basic functionality (describe, etc.)

quiz.q: What does df.isna().mean() return for each column?
opts: The fraction (proportion) of values that are missing
The number of rows that have any NaN
A single True/False for whether the column has any NaN
quiz.why: isna() produces a boolean frame of True/False, which Python treats as 1/0. Taking mean() down each column averages those 1s and 0s, so the result is the proportion of missing values per column, e.g. 0.12 means 12% missing.
quiz.approach: isna().mean() for the missing rate, isna().sum() for the raw count.

quiz.q: During EDA, how do you make value_counts also show how many rows are missing?
opts: It cannot show NaN; you must compute it separately
quiz.why: By default value_counts sets dropna=True and omits NaN entirely, which can hide a large chunk of missing data. Passing dropna=False adds a NaN row to the tally so the missing count is visible right beside the real categories.
quiz.approach: In exploration always pass dropna=False so nothing hides.

quiz.q: In pandas 2.x, calling df.corr() on a DataFrame that still contains text columns will usually:
opts: Raise an error unless you pass numeric_only=True
Automatically ignore the text columns with no change needed
Require you to load a separate SQL engine
quiz.why: Older pandas auto-excluded non-numeric columns from corr(), but pandas 2.x defaults numeric_only=False and attempts to use every column, which fails on strings. Passing numeric_only=True restricts the correlation to numeric columns.
quiz.approach: Add numeric_only=True to corr() (and similar reductions) in pandas 2.x.

lessons.title: Automation and APIs: Pull Data with requests, Safely
lessons.concept:
The last mile of an analyst's leverage is automation: instead of downloading a file by hand every morning, you let a script fetch data straight from an API. An API is just a URL you send a request to, and it replies with structured data, almost always JSON. In Python the requests library does the sending: requests.get(url, params={...}, timeout=10) returns a response object. From there response.json() parses the reply into Python dicts and lists, and pd.json_normalize() flattens those (even nested ones) into a tidy DataFrame you can group and join like any other.

Two lines separate a robust pipeline from a fragile one. First, response.raise_for_status() must come right after every request. A 404 (not found) or 500 (server error) does NOT throw an exception on its own; requests still hands you a response object, and if you skip this check you can end up feeding an HTML error page into your DataFrame and never notice. raise_for_status() converts any 4xx/5xx into a loud HTTPError that stops the job. Second, always pass timeout=; without it a stalled server can hang your script forever with no error at all.

Two more professional habits. Most APIs paginate, returning data in pages of, say, 100 records; you loop, incrementing an offset, until a page comes back empty. And keep secrets out of your code: read the API key from an environment variable (os.environ) rather than hardcoding it, so it never lands in version control or logs. India's open-data portal data.gov.in is a great practice ground, exposing datasets like fuel and commodity prices through a simple api-key-based API.
lessons.example: Instead of manually downloading commodity or fuel-price data from data.gov.in every morning, a 15-line script hits their open API with your api-key, paginates through the records, checks raise_for_status on each call, and lands a clean DataFrame. The report that used to eat your first 30 minutes now runs itself before you reach office.
lessons.code:
import os
import requests
import pandas as pd

BASE = "https://api.data.gov.in/resource/<resource-id>"
API_KEY = os.environ["DATAGOV_KEY"]   # keep secrets OUT of the code

def fetch(offset):
    resp = requests.get(
        BASE,
        params={"api-key": API_KEY, "format": "json",
                "limit": 100, "offset": offset},
        timeout=10,                    # never let a stalled server hang forever
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()            # 4xx/5xx -> raise, stop feeding bad data
    return resp.json()["records"]

# Paginate until the API returns an empty page
rows, offset = [], 0
while True:
    batch = fetch(offset)
    if not batch:
        break
    rows.extend(batch)
    offset += 100

df = pd.json_normalize(rows)           # flatten nested JSON into a flat table
lessons.tryit: Pick any free public JSON API. Write a function that does requests.get with timeout=10, calls raise_for_status(), returns response.json(), and loads the result into a DataFrame with pd.json_normalize. Then deliberately hit a wrong URL and confirm raise_for_status() stops the script.

mistakes.m: Assuming the call succeeded just because requests.get() didn't throw; a 404 or 500 still returns a response object.
mistakes.fix: Call response.raise_for_status() immediately after each request so 4xx/5xx errors halt the pipeline instead of loading an error page into your DataFrame.

mistakes.m: Calling requests.get without a timeout.
mistakes.fix: Always pass timeout= (e.g. 10 seconds). Otherwise a hung or very slow server can block your script indefinitely with no error.

mistakes.m: Hardcoding the API key in the script or the URL and committing it to Git.
mistakes.fix: Read the key from an environment variable via os.environ so it stays out of source control and out of logs.

links.t: data.gov.in: Open Government Data Platform India

quiz.q: What does response.raise_for_status() do?
opts: Raises an HTTPError for 4xx/5xx status codes and does nothing for successful 2xx responses
Automatically retries the request until it succeeds
Returns the numeric status code as an integer
Parses the response body into JSON
quiz.why: A failed HTTP status like 404 or 500 does not by itself raise an exception in requests; you still get a response object. raise_for_status() inspects the status code and raises requests.exceptions.HTTPError for 4xx and 5xx, while passing silently for 2xx, so bad responses stop the pipeline instead of flowing downstream.
quiz.approach: Call raise_for_status() right after every request so errors fail loudly, not silently.

quiz.q: Why should you pass timeout= to requests.get()?
opts: So a hung or very slow server can't freeze your script indefinitely
To make the API respond faster
Because response.json() won't work without it
Because it caches the response for reuse
quiz.why: Without a timeout, requests will wait as long as the server holds the connection open, so a single stalled endpoint can block your entire automated job forever. A timeout makes the call raise after the set number of seconds, which you can then catch and handle.
quiz.approach: Set an explicit timeout on every network call in an unattended script.

quiz.q: You receive a list of nested JSON records and want a flat, analysis-ready table. Best tool?
opts: pd.DataFrame(records), which always fully flattens nesting
quiz.why: pd.json_normalize walks nested dictionaries and expands them into flat columns using dot notation. pd.DataFrame(records) creates columns only for the top-level keys and leaves any nested dicts sitting inside cells as Python objects, which you cannot group or aggregate directly.
quiz.approach: Use json_normalize to flatten nested API responses into a real table.
